description: To repair what is broken or torn, restoring it to functional wholeness. 'Whori' implies care for what exists rather than replacement with new—the ethic of repair that honors the history embedded in objects and the resources required to make them.
sound_symbolism: The word begins with 'who' /ʍo/, the gentle breath of careful attention—approaching what is damaged with tenderness. The round 'o' holds what needs care. Then 'ri' works with rolling action, the repair underway, wholeness returning.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a transitive verb: 'mia wethalu whori.' (I mend the garment.) Related to 'theala' (heal), which applies to living beings; 'whori' applies to objects. Compare 'shiroka' (repair), which emphasizes technical restoration of function.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Mending extends object life, reduces waste, honors embedded resources. Repair culture counters throwaway culture.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Traditional cultures mended everything—clothes, tools, relationships. Skill in repair was essential knowledge.
